content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for "Guy Ritchie" movies from other directors

**Score:** 73
**Body:** Chaotic action comedy, basically. We have this running gag that we refer to these movies as "Guy Ritchie movie directed by someone else", hence the title.

I don't think I have to elaborate more, but just to exclude a few: rock n rolla, lock stock, snatch, bullet train, crank, trainspotting, hot fuzz, layer cake, the nice guys etc.

UPDATE: of course I have seen a great majority of the recommended movies, and then there are the replies that are just straight up from my list above, but with all that said I'm happy to see that the post got traction. I got movies for weeks, and random future people who get here from google should have movies for months!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Usual Suspects, Lucky Number Sleven, Seven Psychopaths, Burn After Reading
  - Seconded for Lucky Number Sleven.

  
Love that movie.
2. Kingsmen: The secret service.

In Bruges.
  - In Bruges is just a brilliant movie
3. Go (1999)
4. Caught Stealing (2025)
5. Baby Driver, maybe?
6. In Bruges
7. Guns Akimbo
8. Shoot em up

## 2. I am looking for a series or movie whose events take place in a cold location and includes storms

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I’m not a fan of the horror or romance genres, so I don’t want suggestions from those categories, or at least the horror or romance shouldn’t be a main element in the series or movie. The genre doesn’t matter, and it’s not required for all episodes to have storms or winter, but it would be better if most of them do. It’s also preferable for a group of people to be forced to stay in one place during that, but it’s not a requirement

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Hateful Eight (film) 

True Detective: Night Country (TV series - season 4, but it’s an anthology so you don’t have to watch the prior seasons)
  - Ooooh I forgot The Hateful Eight !
2. The Ice Storm
3. The Day After Tomorrow 

White Fang

Cliffhanger 

Vertical Limit
4. "The Grey" (2011)

"The Empire Strikes Back" (1980) Original Theatrical Version

"Whiteout" (2009)

"No Exit" (2022)

"Fargo" (1996)

"Fire and Ice" (1983)

"The Mountain Between Us" (2017)
5. Ice Station Zebra. Takes place during the coldwar.
6. \- Arctic (2018)  
Pure survival in freezing conditions. Storms, whiteouts, isolation — exactly what you’re looking for.

\- The Revenant  
Not a “storm all the time” type of film, but it has some intense winter wilderness survival scenes.

\- Wind River  
Crime thriller in a snowy, isolated Native American reservation. Strong winter setting throughout.

\- The Finest Hour  
Based on a real rescue mission during one of the worst winter storms ever. Cold, claustrophobic, lots of storm intensity.
7. Stephen King's Storm of the Century
8. Snowpiercer (2013 movie)
9. Not a fan of horror but I’m going to recommend a horror movie anyway

The Thing (1982)

## 3. I'm depressed please suggest me a movie

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I have issues related to career and my life. Can people of reddit suggest me some good movies? I really need some positive energy. So I can restart working on myself.

language: English

Please mention some details(not spoilers) too. It'll be helpful and you can also suggest movies in languages other than English.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big fish
2. Office Space
3. Crazy Stupid Love
4. Hunt for the Wilderpeople.
5. silver linings playbook 👌
6. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
The Goat Life (Indian)
  - Upvote for Mitty. Gets me everytime.
7. Goonies

StarDust 

Princess Bride

Neverending Story
8. Depends whether you want hope or distraction.

Hope: my favourite, The Castle (1997), a film about a family who take on the Australian government in order to keep their home. It's a classic in this country and is super funny and has a lot of soul! 

Distraction: Sherlock Jr (1924). A silent film but not a bore. A Buster Keaton slapstick comedy where the main character falls asleep at a movie theatre and gets into wacky hijinks in his dreams!
9. Good Will Hunting

## 4. hey dudes heres my situation.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** So i guess i can say i judged a book by its cover. Last night i watched the movie heathers for the first time. A movie i have seen around and would otherwise not have watched at first glance. I only watched it bcs of a wynona rider interview i was watching lastnight aswell. Basically its on of the best movies ive everwatched and has made it to my list of grails. I guess i like cult classics. Movies like the craft, mean girls, legally blonde, breakfast club, the goonies, mid 90s, if anyone can relate or understand id really love some more movies like that

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. But I'm a Cheerleader
2. Brick
3. Heathers was a lot edgier than most teen films at the time. You might like these...

* Drop Dead Gorgeous
* Election (1999)
* Ginger Snaps
* Serial Mom
* The River's Edge
4. Pump Up the Volume (1990)

Heathers (1989)
5. The Lost Boys
6. Empire Records
7. Breakfast Club and Goonies are not cult classics by any stretch. I think they are both considered quintessential 80s films. 

That being said if you want a cult classic in a similar vein to Goonies, the Monster Squad is better imho
8. Jennifers Body - If you consider a cult classic not from the 90s. I feel like it is imo
9. Jawbreaker

Almost Famous
10. Better Off Dead

## 5. Help me find some comedies!

**Score:** 21
**Body:** So my fiancée and I watch a movie pretty much every single day. We’re huge horror fans, but also just kind of love movies in a general way. 

Anyway, we’ve been trying to find some funnier stuff to watch bc we’ve been watching either very serious movies, or horror stuff. Which can be funny in its own way but we need to branch out a little. 

Some of our favorite funny movies are Best In Show, Bottoms, They Came Together. Idk if that helps at all. But thanks ahead of time!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What’s Up Doc
2. If you like Best In Show, check out Waiting for Guffman and A Mighty Wind.
3. Hilarious oldie—Young Frankenstein
  - Any of the Mel Brooks comedies- Space Balls, Blazing Saddles, Robin Hood: Men in Tights, Dracula: Dead and Loving It...

The Blues Brothers 

Death at a Funeral (both versions)
4. What We Do in the Shadows
5. Clue

What About Bob?

Wet Hot American Summer

The House
6. Have you seen the movie “What We Do In The Shadows” ?  Maybe the best horror comedy ever made.  And there have been some good ones.

Also ;

- Ghostbusters 1&2

- Young Frankenstein

- little Shop of Horrors

- The Frighteners

- Rocky Horror Picture Show

- The Burbs

- Army of Darkness
7. Drop Dead Gorgeous
8. Hundreds of Beavers 

Windy City Heat
9. Little Miss Sunshine

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Characters that are not meant to be a villain but act like a villain

**Score:** 718
**Body:** Example 1: Grandpa Joe from Charlie and the Chocolate Factory:

* He’s been faking being bedridden for 20+ years.  The moment Charlie finds the Golden Ticket, Grandpa Joe suddenly leaps out of bed, sings, dances, and is sprinting around like a 30-year-old.
* That said, he's a freeloader, refusing to work to help his family.  
* He manipulates Charlie into using the golden ticket instead of selling it for a fortune and helping their family.
* He insists on being Charlie’s plus-one to the factory, even though any of the adults who actually work to support the family would be more deserving.
* He’s the one who encourages Charlie to steal Fizzy Lifting Drinks and almost gets them both disqualified forever

Grandpa Joe is a lazy, selfish, manipulative moocher who only gets out of bed when there’s personal gain involved, nearly ruins Charlie’s life multiple times, and faces zero consequences because Charlie is too pure-hearted to notice.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. George Clooney’s character from O’ Brother Where Art Thou. He lied to the boys so he could escape from the prison farm to stop his wife from marrying a decent man.
  - He's bona fide.
  - Tbf, her suitor is trying to get a grand wizard of the Klan elected governor. He may not have known, but still...
2. Robin Williams in _Mrs. Doubtfire_ - Sally Field very reasonably divorced that lunatic yet somehow she‘s the bad one because he isn‘t allowed to see their children because of her?
  - Him dressing up as the nanny and being in the house under false pretenses pretty much proves he’s unhinged and why he lost custody in the first place
  - You're wrong. He wasn't denied custody. He was denied custody TEMPORARILY!!!

They said they'd reevaluate in 3 months to reassess him and see if he's stable enough to have custody.

That makes his actions look worse. He did all this because he couldn't wait 3 months.

Though it was a dick move of her to arrive an hour early then tell them to go. That's very minor sure, but that's 1 thing I think she did that deserves scrutiny.
  - How many qualified candidates did HE get rid of in order to rig him being hired for the gig??

And all he had to do was be responsible. That man had a circus at their San Francisco house and tried to kill Pierce Brosnan with spices he was allergic to.
3. Maverick, Top Gun

I saw a YouTube clip of a JAG estimating he would spend more than 20 years in military prison for all the shit he did.  He would have lost his wings the second he disobeyed a direct order, 10 mins into the movie
  - I feel like that’s something the audience is supposed to pick up on and that’s why in the sequel Iceman is a 4 Star Admiral while Maverick is still a Captain.
4. The bartender from Three Amigos is a sociopath. Anyone who tells you tequila is “like beer” then willingly serves it to you, knowing what it truly is, is a super villain.

## 2. Why was Michael Biehn not a bigger star?

**Score:** 654
**Body:** He plays two of the most memorable characters in the most legendary movies ever. What happened? Not to mention bro was quite good looking. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I remember him most as Johnny Ringo in Tombstone
  - You must be ringo. Look darlin, Johnny Ringo.
    - Should I hate him?
      - You dont even know him
      - I don’t know. There’s just…somethin about him..
  - Why, Johnny Ringo, you look like someone just walked over your grave.
2. Alot of things. LIFE, etc.

He was something else as Johnny Ringo in Tombstone. Seeing him in Planet Terror was really cool.

Coinciding with the decline of his acting career during the 1990s, Biehn suffered from **alcoholism** until the mid-2000s. Biehn suffered a [**stroke**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stroke) around 2008 and had [**open heart surgery**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardiac_surgery). When questioned on why he did not attain stardom despite having lead roles in well-regarded hit films like *The Terminator* and *Aliens*, Biehn told the *Hollywood Reporter* "People always talk about me being an '80s star. I was not an '80s star. [Bruce Willis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bruce_Willis) was an '80s star. [Tom Cruise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tom_Cruise) was an '80s star.  [Schwarzenegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger) and [Stallone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone). [Mel Gibson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mel_Gibson). Those guys were making $20 million \[a picture\]. I never even got $1 million. I kind of liked it that way."

Biehn remains financially comfortable due to his [Screen Actors Guild](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild) pension. James Cameron suggested that "what held Michael back was that he didn't like playing the Hollywood game — schmoozing people and all that. For him it was all about the work". **Biehn has never hired a publicist and was reportedly never interested in the "fame aspect of the business, even though his agent — and Cameron — both believed he had what it took to be a major star".**
  - Idk if you've seen the mandolorian but he was so good in the ahsoka episode. He just has insane screen presence to where you really hope he becomes part of mandos team by the end of the episode.
  - Him not wanting to play the Hollywood game makes me like him even more.
3. I met him outside of a sushi spot with my dad in the early 2000’s. Incredibly nice and down to earth man. 

Him and my dad were talking and my dad goes, “you look familiar.” 

And he goes, “I’m an actor.” 

“What’s your name?” 

“Michael Biehn.” 

And my dad nudges my arm and goes, “terminator!” 

And then my dad tells Michael Biehn that he’s a Grip in the studios and Biehn shakes his hand. End of story. I was a kid, but he was still incredibly nice and cool.

## 3. What is your favorite NON action or horror SciFi movie?

**Score:** 433
**Body:** SciFi is by far my favorite genre. However, rarely do you come across SciFi movies that aren’t action packed with guns, monsters, aliens, space craft, diseased or robots. 

I would definitely enjoy more “leveled” scifi movies of the thriller variety. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gattaca is so underrated. I remember watching it in my biology class and being one of the only people who liked it.
  - Clearly you were educated amongst cretins
2. Moon (2009)
  - I'm a sucker for all things Sam Rockwell. Especially if he gets to dance.
3. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
4. Ex Machina (2014)
5. A Scanner Darkly
6. Arrival
  - i love Arrival - the way it explores language, time, and human connection really stuck with me. The scene where Louise finally understands the alien language gave me chills
7. The Truman Show (1998). 

Really compelling story and Jim Carrey is such a great actor

## 4. I don't think there will ever be a better action movie than "The Rock".

**Score:** 274
**Body:** It really is a 10/10 action movie in my opinion. Probably one of the best (and most honorable) bad guys ever (Ed Harris), who was a (THE) total bad-ass in this movie. Nic Cage was, well, Nic Cage. Cheesy lines, but the hero that saves the day. Sean Connery in probably his last role where he just fucks shit up and it is still somewhat believable (him being like 70-something when the movie came out). And a freaking stacked supporting cast that ALL delivered top-notch performances that you just don't see in movies these days. It is pure adrenaline and entertainment from the moment it starts to the moment it ends. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Definitely Bay’s best film! He can do well with a great script.
  - Whaddya say we cut the chit-chat, A-hole!
    - I legit heard this in Nick Cage's voice and laughed. 

"Tell me... how in the name of Zeus's BUTTHOLE, did you get out of your cell!?!?"
2. You forgot the best part, Michael fuckin Biehn
  - I WILL NOT GIVE THAT ORDER!!!!!!
    - WHAT THE HELL IS WRONG WITH YOU MAN!!!
3. Secret bond film
4. "I'll take pleasure in guttin you... Boy"
5. You've been around a lot of corpses. Is that normal?

What, the feet thing?

Yeah, the feet thing.

Yeah, it happens.

Well I'm having a hard time concentrating. Can you do something about it?

Like what, kill him again?
6. T2 and Die Hard

## 5. King of Vampire Movies

**Score:** 237

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Joel Schumacher - "So I've got an idea for a vampire movie. It's about this group of punks that live in a small beach town in California, but they're actually vampires. I've got this great vision for a five minute scene where a heavily oiled man plays the saxophone in a provactive manner to a large crowd of people at a local fair." 

Producer - "And this heavily oiled man is one of the vampires, and he has the crowd in a spell with his music before feeding on them?"

Schumacher - "No. He finishes his song, the crowd applauds and we never see him again."

Producer - "You son of a bitch, Schumacher....another bullseye. Have another line."
  - Sergio!
  - And Schumacher made the right decision and we'll always be grateful for it.
    - *I still believe!* 🎷🎷🎷
      - It's iconic for a reason. An 80's rock tune with a killer sax riff that fits the aesthetic of the movie to a tee.

The entire soundtrack is iconic.
2. They’re not noodles Michael
3. Dracula-oldman
4. Fright Night
5. Near Dark was so much better.

Bill Paxton was a cooler vampire.
6. No one has ever made a saxophone look cooler. I cant believe OP didnt include a picture.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Blue is the Warmest Color and The Handmaiden

**Score:** 17
**Body:** These were both films directed by straight men featuring lesbian relationships and very explicit scenes. While they both were met with critical acclaim upon release, the appreciation for Blue is the Warmest Color has dwindled over time, while appreciation for The Handmaiden has only grown. I wanted to get people's perspective on why this might be. What do you think sets them apart?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Probably has something to do with the actresses of Blue is the Warmest Color openly saying the director was manipulative and made them feel like prostitutes: https://www.pearlanddean.com/lea-seydoux-still-angry-with-blue-is-the-warmest-colour-director-over-alleged-exploitation/

Not to mention other sexual assault allegations against the director: https://www.theguardian.com/film/2018/oct/31/blue-is-the-warmest-colour-director-abdellatif-kechiche

The Handmaiden was directed by Park Chan-Wook, who remains an exciting cinematic voice, and hasn’t been accused of abuse (to my knowledge).
  - Also in the graphic novel Blue is based off, there’s no sex. So the film going as extreme as it did just seemed exploitative
    - That’s not true! There is sex in the book, just not as graphic and long as the movie.
      - The director really added his own gooner brained interpretation of women having sex.
        - I agree to some degree but first time I watched it having no context on who directed it and stuff I found the sex scene very relatable as a lesbian….and so did my girlfriend ahah. I guess it depends tho now I can see it better
          - As a straight(ish) male with no direct personal experience of these things, I've always found it fascinating that lesbian commentary on this movie's sex scenes ranges from "no woman has ever done that or would ever have the slightest interest in doing that" to "that's really hot, I did it with my wife last night."
(I'll also say that the sex scenes are not really what I remember about it, at all. I remember the early scenes in the school, the first trip to the lesbian bar, the awkward dinner with the parents, the tension with the artistic friend group who don't think much of their friend's young girlfriend... But the sex scenes are all anyone ever seems to talk about.)
2. In my perspective I think it has to do with the sex scenes in Blue is the Warmest Color not being much of a part in the story while in the Maidmaiden sexuality as a whole is a big theme and sex scenes are used for symbolic purposes. Also in the latter no men where in the room when the sex scenes were filmed so there was not any men watching them perform
3. The Handmaiden is a better film overall, and has more going on in the plot and with the characters outside of the relationship. There are more things to chew on, and I believe they’re both adults in the movie, while in Blue is the Warmest Color, they start off as teens, which makes the some of the sex scenes feel grosser
4. I have not seen BitWC, but I believe the main issue was complaints from the actors about how the sex scenes were directed and handled. That naturally colors the critical perception. 


I liked The Handmaiden a lot, but I found the sex scenes, for the most part, lame and very "directed by a man who knows nothing about lesbian sex." That drove down the quality of the movie a lot for me. 


If anybody liked Handmaiden a lot, the book it's based on, Fingersmith, is incredible, and there's a great BBC miniseries adaptation. 
  - > I liked The Handmaiden a lot, but I found the sex scenes, for the most part, lame and very "directed by a man who knows nothing about lesbian sex."

I loved the movie, but I think it's in the first major sex scene where they're really going at it and the music is swelling and triumphant, and then the girls clap their hands together as the music climaxes in a moment of "Hell yeah! Lesbian sex is awesome!".  Oh man, that had me dying of laughter.

## 2. Footage from Napoleon 1927 in a different film

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I am trying to find a film which i recently saw. I completely forgot what it was called and what it was about and now I cannot find it anyhow, AI cannot help me either. The film uses footage from “Napoleon” by Abel Gans to highlight the inner feelings of the protagonist. The footage of Napoleon’s face from the original silent film appears several times. Not sure, but the film could have been about a queer person and I think it is recent. I would really appreciate any hints on what kind of film it could be! Thanks a lot! 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It may be High & Low the documentary on John Galliano which makes visual parallels throughout of that film and his creative and egotistical rise and fall. It’s directed by Kevin MacDonald of Touching The Void fame.
  - OMG yes thank you so much! Interestingly it is not mentioned on Napoleon page on IMDB
    - You should add it! :D
      - Yass will do
    - You’re very welcome.

## 3. How do you find recent art films?

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I've seen a lot of mid-century art film but I've been struggling to seem relevant and cool lately... I need some more recent ones.--I look for them; it is quite hard to find them. Part industry, part critical problem, a greater part, I think, due to my own laziness. 

By "art film" I suppose I just mean: good movie. But there are pretty high standards for that. Yadda yadda, Hollywood in decline, the complaints about modern film are excessively financialized and miss out on what's actually important. Anyway. 

I really don't know where to look for the good stuff, mostly because it doesn't seem to win awards like it used to. Or get much press coverage? Most of the good movies I've seen in recent years I've come across basically by accident, like Totem (2021). That was a good movie; I am not sure how I even know about it. Whenever I try to watch a Cannes or Berlin winner, I'm disappointed (like with All We Imagine as Light.) So I have to imagine a lot of the really good movies are, like Totem, hidden among the nominees, winners of more minor awards, or national submission to big competitions that didn't make the cut. Or didn't get anywhere at all! There seems to have been a shift in the critical attitude towards what is called "highbrow" film; I can't articulate what exactly this shift consists in. There has also been a shift in directorial focus, equally obscure to me. What has caused what?--Or, it is a more general shift in attitudes towards filmmaking. 

It is hard to think of a movie made in the long 21st century (mid 80s onward, of course) that's as good as something like Celine and Julie Go Boating, or... etc. etc. That's a somewhat annoying attitude to have. Something of the spirit is still out there, right?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Firstly, I don't think framing this in terms of "art movies" is going to help. Everyone has a basic idea of what an "art movie" is, and we all basically agree that it excludes big studio blockbusters, but beyond that, I don't think there's any consensus. Your definition seems to be, "If I like it, it's an art movie; if I don't, it's not". That's refreshingly honest, but it doesn't help anyone else to pinpoint the movies you're talking about or to recommend ways of finding them. 

So really this is just a problem of how to find movies that you like. Lots of cinephiles have that problem, regardless of what kinds of movies they prefer, and especially if their tastes are a bit more niche. The answer - which you might not like - is to put in the work. Watch a lot of movies until you find one that you like. When you do, take note of where you found it and anything else that might indicate where similar movies might be found (Was it screening at a particular festival? Google it - where did it get reviewed? Do the reviews mention other, similar filmmakers?) Repeat. 

Eventually, you might start to notice patterns - maybe some festivals have a better hit rate for you than others; maybe there's a film magazine/website that reviews a lot of things worth checking out; maybe there's a micro-scene of Bolivian art students making exactly the kinds of films you want to see. The more you watch, the more you'll get an idea of what your tastes actually are, and how to cater for them. And then you won't need to use vague terms like "art movie" to search for newer stuff.
2. I follow critics who attend festivals, it's mainly through their recommendations that I discover good films and directors these days.

I also pay a lot of attention to the official selection of festivals, (not just the award-winning ones) especially the big ones in Europe especially Berlin, which is the festival with the most diverse selection.
3. The London Review of Books' regular section "At the movies" comes to mind. It's not really focused on art-movies and it's a lot of text, might be helpful for input nevertheless. 

https://www.lrb.co.uk/search-results?dateFrom=&dateTo=&oldsort=relevance&any=&all=&phrase=&exclude=&search=at%20the%20movies&page=1&sort=newest


You might also feed your taste to letterboxd.com ... and see what comes of it, if you're willing to do the clicky-work :-)
4. I don't think it's possible to keep up with the number of films that are released around the world every year these days. I tend to keep an eye on a few websites that discuss the type of films I like. We have a cinema not too far away that shows zero blockbusters and they focus on more cerebral movies. 

You have to put in the work, unfortunately. But fortunately the world is at your fingertips these days!
5. I agree that I can't quite figure put what kind of movies you do and don't like based on this. But the easiest way I've found new movies is through Letterboxd.


Go to a movie you like (like Totem) and find a review you agree with. Click on that person's profile and scroll through their other liked or 5-star films. If they've got similar taste, give them a follow and you'll be able to see future reviews and activity. Repeat. Soon you've got a community of like-minded movie fans sharing recs and reviews. 


Also worth looking into the Sight and Sound list from 2022. Critics name their top 10 movies and Sight and Sound compiles it in a giant list. See which critics voted for Celine and Julie and search for the other 9 movies they contributed to the list.


It's interest that you mention Celine and. Julie, which won an award at the Locarno Festival but didn't screen at Cannes. It was also difficult to find for a long time. Sometimes excellent movies take years (or decades) to get recognized. 
6. Criterion Channel is great for that, I’ve never regretted subscribing – and they have Black Friday sales, just saying. Ridiculously cheap for the price, and they have premieres a few times a month, so you get new films dropping that of course you can stream but you can also watch the premiere, they often have an interview with the director as well or something like that that makes it a bit of an event.

Recent premieres include  All We Imagine As Light, Cloud, Caught By the Tides, The Shrouds and Union— all of them qualifies art films by your definition!
7. Feels like you’re searching for a particular flair or experience. The movies you mention have a sense of playfulness and intimacy, narrative freedom, dreamlike logic, and a directorial presence you can feel through the screen. That’s what I get from the descriptions and visuals, and maybe some knowledge of Rivette. I’d suggest diving into French or Czech cinema – binge Rivette and see which of his films resonate with you, and search for modern filmmakers which were inspired by him.

A practical approach might also be to look at which festival section a film appears in and explore other movies from that same section; for example, La Forastera by Lucía Aleñar Iglesias was in TIFF’s Discovery section this year and felt very much in this spirit. You could also check previous years’ Discovery selections to find similar overlooked or under-the-radar films.
8. Keeping an eye on the big festivals (Sundance, Cannes, Toronto International Film Festival, Vancouver International Film Festival) are where a lot of the great films come out. I also recommend just looking at what is playing at your local theaters (assuming they ever play anything other than the big studio movies) because you can find a fair amount of movies under your radar just by doing that.
9. I think you're getting some good suggestions about letterboxd, festivals, other websites or publications, etc. Something that could also help is checking out or following on social media distributors/production companies that make smaller more arty or intimate movies. Companies like Mubi, Neon, Criterion.

Not sure where you're from but national film boards or similar organizations might also release these kind of movies or promote movies being made that aren't blockbusters. I follow the national film board of Canada on YouTube, I assume similar exist for other anglo countries if you want to watch things in English and I assume there's subtitled films available from other countries as well.

## 4. Need Help Picking 3 "Must See" Films from a European Film Festival Lineup

**Score:** 0
**Body:** A group of cinemas in my city is hosting a European Film Festival, and from what I understand, they’ll be showing the most important European films released this year. Honestly, I’m not very familiar with European cinema, I’ve probably watched around 10 European films in my entire life (hunt, The Intouchables, amelie etc... the very famous ones like those), but I’ve always wanted to get into it more. Every time I try to watch a European film, though, I get this feeling that it’s too “heavy,” and I end up going back to Hollywood my comfort zone. So I feel like this festival is the perfect opportunity that just fell into my lap. The only issue is that because of college, I only have time to watch about three films. So I’d really appreciate it if fans of European cinema could tell me which three films from the lineup are the most "must see"s.

1. A Poet
2. A Private Life
3. Adam’s Sake
4. Christy
5. DJ Ahmet
6. Dreams (Sex Love)
7. Father Mother Sister Brother
8. Gavagai
9. It Was Just an Accident

14. Nino

16. Sirat
17. Sleepless City
18. Straight Circle
19. The Love That Remains
20. The Luminous Life
21. White Snail
22. Zafzifa



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The big two here are Sentimental Value and It Was Just an Accident, those are gonna be in play come award season.

Father Mother Sister Brother I’ve heard great things about, same for Sirat and La Grazia, that last one by one pf my favourite directors (Sorrentino).
2. I think the easier ones to get into are Late Shift (you follow a nurse working one night in an understaffed hospital, it's very immersive and tension inducing), It was just an accident (it's a dark comedy, the movie is very political but also entertaining) and Sentimental Value (it's a quiet family drama, but some parts involving Elle Fanning are in English), Dreams (it is basically seeing the diary of a teen falling in love, I didn't see the other two movies of the loose trilogy and it didn't impact my enjoyment at all).
3. Some of the films are very American, like Father Mother Sister Brother by Jim Jarmusch. I have not seen all but 2 of the films in the lineup. Father Brother Sister Brother and Sirat (the films I have seen) are very delicate films and masterpieces. Even my grandmother surprisingly has loved Sirat. However, they may require patience.
4. I thought dreams (sex love) was amazing but it apart of a trilogy (sex and love being the other films) they aren't related narrative wise but follow similar themes. Might want to see if you can see the others first as I feel like they get better after each movie but if you cant maybe give it a shot.

Also if christy is the Irish film and not the American boxing one then I'd HIGHLY recommend it, its also very funny.
5. I really enjoyed "A Poet" (not a European film, Colombian).

Sirat is pretty buzzy and an engaging watch (Jury Prize at Cannes) but should get wider distribution (same with Sentimental Value and It Was Just an Accident).

Father Mother Sister Brother is from the US but I'm awful fond of Jim Jarmusch.

## 5. Movies where eroticism doesn't dampen the story

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I was curious of any movies which are more known for being erotic or have graphic sex scenes that overshadow an otherwise good plot, performances or cinematography. The main example I was thinking would be **Blue is the Warmest Colour**, most widely known for having graphic sex scenes, it also has a very raw depiction of relationship struggles and the issues that queer people can face when discovering their sexuality.

The performance by Adele Exarchopoulos still remains in my mind one of the best put to screen in the past few decades, and yet if you just heard about the movie you'd possibly think it was more or less a porn.

What other movies are there like it? The same level of acting buried beneath a seemingly lewd facade. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The films that immediately pop into my head are Ang Lee’s Lust Caution and Park Chan Wook’s The Handmaiden. Both of which embrace sensuality and sexuality as integral drivers of the narrative.
2. I love "lewd" movies. Sexuality is arguably the most complicated, essential element of our humanity. Regardless of anyone's personal relationship with it, whether someone is sexually active or asexual, hetero or homo or bi or pan, it is at the center of our sense of identity, our politics, our philosophies... it's the sole reason why any of us exist at all.

So why wouldn't our films be about sex? Kubrick, one of history's greatest filmmakers, ended his career with Eyes Wide Shut. There's also David Lynch's Blue Velvet, David Cronenberg's Crash, those are three off the top of my head.
  - And sometimes sexy things can just be for fun! You don't have to balance erotic scenes with serious deep character arcs and heavy themes to "justify" those scenes, nor do those scenes always "ruin" or "distract" from other things that happen in the movie. Sometimes the only good thing about the movie is that it is sexy!

It's okay for movies to make you feel horny sometimes!
    - See: Flash Gordon
    - The whole overreaction against sexuality (or even just plain nudity) feels very puritanical, a uniquely American mental disorder.

For me, "Swimming Pool" (2003) is a good movie, but perhaps the most sensual part of it doesn't even explicitly have sex - it's the part near the end when Sarah seduces the groundskeeper.
3. Do the two Lars Von Trier movies, Nymphomaniac volumes one and two count?  There’s a good bit of sexuality in a lot of his movies, but certainly they don’t generally come across as titillating.

And wow, Breaking the Waves (1996) is simply horrific in it’s use of sexuality.
4. This idea that explicit scenes or even suggestive scenes would somehow be detrimental to a story by default has always surprised me, let alone a piece of art: does it not immediately invalidate queer art exploring one of the key aspects of the queer experience? Would Querelle not be a lesser film if sexual content was removed? Would Marlon Riggs' films not lose half of its intersectional perspective if all explicit implications were cut? Even in Ming-Liang Tsai's films, where the queer experience is less pronounced but always a constant, would its impact not be lessened without its queer expression? These are films where eroticism and sexuality *are* parts of the story, and that doesn't even get us into the vast landscape of cult sexploitation filmmakers like Franco or Wishman where nudity and sex are not only parts of the story but content in its own right.

This idea of scenes depicting sex being 'unnecessary' seems to me a recent gripe, straddling both conservative prudishness and progressive feminism: mainstream cinema undoubtedly suffers from a male gaze that even predates Laura Mulvey's seminal essay, something that's only exacerbated as the content depicted becomes more explicit; depictions like the gratuitous ones in Blue Is The Warmest Color obviously fit this bill. However, the black-and-white perspective that any depiction is a detriment to narrative seems less concerned with its depiction and more of a prudish view of sexuality and sexual expression as abnormal and abject, fully in line with a growing societal conservativism and embrace of 'traditional' structures and expression. Is not sexuality a key aspect of human opposition to a death drive, a near-universal experience who's depiction predates all of us able to post in this thread? It is one thing to oppose the form of its depiction, another to opposite it in its entirety.
5. That's really interesting, I would personally give Blue Is The Warmest Color as my primary example of the opposite. The sex scenes drag down the movie horribly, the personalities of these beautiful characters just disappear and suddenly they're in scenes that are lit and shot like they're meant to be uploaded to the lesbian section of PornHub. It is clear to me that there weren't any queer women with input on the sex in that movie.


But to answer your question, I think the sex in Don't Look Now is a big part of what makes that movie work. Plus there was a big controversy at the time because they're so realistic that people still speculate on whether or not Julie Christie and Donald Sutherland we're actually doing it.
  - I *think* that’s what OP is saying, but I had to reread a few times — that Blue Is The Warmest Color is an excellent movie which *is* sort of dampened by its lewdness
    - Oh yeah, maybe. Good point. If so, titling the post "Movies where eroticism doesn't dampen the story" and then start the second sentence "The main example I was thinking" is very confusing. 


Re-reading it, it seems like maybe they're asking more about a film's lewd reputation, rather than how the sex actually influences the movie. I guess Don't Look Now still works for that
